goal: Add three context-efficient file editing tools (grep_files, replace_in_file, append_file) to the Go fileserver REST API, the Python MCP wrapper, and .mcp.json — all respecting the os.OpenRoot /workspace jail.

tasks.name: Add 3 REST endpoints to Go fileserver
tasks.action: In fileserver/main.go, add three new HTTP handler functions and register their routes: (1) POST /grep — accepts JSON body {pattern, max_results?}, walks the os.OpenRoot-jailed /workspace tree, returns [{file, line_number, line}] up to max_results (default 100); (2) POST /replace — accepts {path, old_string, new_string}, reads file via OpenRoot, replaces all occurrences of old_string with new_string, writes back, returns {replacements_made}; fail with 4xx if replacements_made == 0; (3) POST /append — accepts {path, content}, opens file via OpenRoot for append, writes content, returns {bytes_written}. All three must use the existing os.OpenRoot root handle (never open files outside /workspace). In fileserver/main_test.go, add unit tests for each handler covering: happy path, zero-match failure for replace, max_results truncation for grep, and path-traversal rejection.
tasks.verify: Run `go test ./fileserver/...` — all existing tests still pass and new tests for grep_files, replace_in_file, and append_file pass.
tasks.done: `go test ./fileserver/...` exits 0 with no failures, and `go vet ./fileserver/...` reports no issues.

tasks.name: Add 3 MCP tool functions to files_mcp.py
tasks.action: In files_mcp.py, add three new MCP tool definitions and handler functions mirroring the new REST endpoints: (1) grep_files(pattern: str, max_results: int = 100) → POST /grep, returns formatted results as 'file:lineno: line' strings; (2) replace_in_file(path: str, old_string: str, new_string: str) → POST /replace, returns the replacements_made count; (3) append_file(path: str, content: str) → POST /append, returns bytes_written. Follow the exact same pattern as existing tools (error handling, HTTPS to mcp-server:8443, MCP_API_TOKEN auth header). In test_files_mcp.py, add unit tests for each new function: mock the HTTPS call, assert correct endpoint/payload, assert correct return value, and assert HTTPError propagation.
tasks.verify: Run `python -m pytest test_files_mcp.py -v` — all existing tests still pass and new tests for grep_files, replace_in_file, and append_file pass.
tasks.done: `python -m pytest test_files_mcp.py` exits 0 with no failures and the three new tool names appear in the MCP server's tool list when files_mcp.py is run with `--list` or equivalent introspection.

tasks.name: Register the 3 new tools in .mcp.json
tasks.action: Open .mcp.json and add entries for grep_files, replace_in_file, and append_file under the fileserver MCP server's tool list, following the exact same schema/format used by the five existing fileserver tools (read_workspace_file, list_files, create_file, write_file, delete_file). Include correct input schema definitions: grep_files needs {pattern: string, max_results?: integer}, replace_in_file needs {path: string, old_string: string, new_string: string}, append_file needs {path: string, content: string}.
tasks.verify: Run `python -m json.tool .mcp.json` to confirm valid JSON, then run the existing integration test that checks MCP registration (test.sh step 7) and confirm all three new tool names appear.
tasks.done: .mcp.json is valid JSON, contains entries for grep_files, replace_in_file, and append_file, and the integration test that validates MCP tool registration exits 0.
